title: M5 MacBook Pro Teardown | Apple’s Subtle Step Forward in Repairability
transcript: The MacBook Pro M5 looks the same, feels the same,  and—spoiler—it mostly is the same as the M4. But   inside, some subtle changes tell an interesting  story about how Apple’s designing for repair. The new M5 equipped 14” MacBook Pros offer few  new features other than a modest performance bump,   a new architecture which doubles the  maximum storage options from 2TB to   4TB and a substantial increase in SSD  read/write speeds and memory bandwidth. Granted, that’s no small achievement, but most  people already running on M-series silicon may   find little reason to upgrade. And with Apple  leaning in to repair manuals, tools, and parts,   it’s never been a better time to skip the  upgrade and fix the device you already own. So the big question remaining from  this year's big M5 announcement is:   did Apple upgrade the MacBook Pro’s repairability? Let’s find out. Teardown The process for opening up the MacBook Pros  has barely changed over the past few years.   We still have P5 screws securing the bottom  cover and it’s still clipped in place. What has changed is Apple’s approach  to repair. With the availability of   repair guides on day one, I can see  Apple’s recommended approach for   making the device safe is to remove the  Battery Management System’s cable first. This cuts power to the rest of the device,   even as the battery is connected, preventing  any accidental short circuits while I continue   to dispatch the trackpad flex cable  that sits over the battery connector. With the BMS board screws removed,   I can disconnect the battery by  folding back the battery connector tab. The guides aren’t perfect, and Apple’s  design for repair still has room for   improvement. The battery replacement  process is a perfect example of this. As of right now, Apple only sells the battery  preinstalled in the main chassis that also   contains the keyboard. That means the official  battery replacement process requires removing   everything—and I mean everything—to replace the  single consumable component inside this device. Pull tabs might be tedious to  deal with when they’re fresh,   but they become an outright frustration as the  adhesive hardens over time. If you’re lucky,   you’ll hear the signature pull tab  squeaks…followed by a satisfying snap. If not, well, you’ll need some isopropyl  alcohol and some patience to get through   all 12 pull tabs. The good news is that  for the first time in a MacBook Pro,   you don’t have to remove the trackpad to  access the pull tabs on the central battery   cells. It’s an iterative upgrade for repair  too it seems, but it’s one I’ll happily take. Now we can take a look at the 6-cell battery  rated for 72.6 Wh. That’s a touch more than   the 72.4 Wh in the M4 MacBook Pro. Both  are less than the 80 Wh battery we found   in our ROG Xbox Ally X teardown, which if  you missed you can watch on our channel. Remember what I said about Apple’s way of battery  replacement being unreasonable? Stick around till   the end of this teardown and I’ll show you exactly  how far you’d have to go following their guide. Moving on to my next victim, I can see from  Apple’s guides that both speakers are held   in place by glue, so I’ll disconnect the speaker  flex cables, apply some isopropyl alcohol around   the edges, and slide a pick underneath  to wedge each of these components out. Another component that’s immediately accessible at  this stage is the lid angle sensor. It’s a simple   process to remove, held in place by a couple  of torx screws and a single Pentalobe P2 screw. Unfortunately despite the modular nature of  this laptop, with the exception of the trackpad,   there aren’t any other components left  to remove without first getting into the   complicated business of removing the logic board. It’s a bit of a process, but once that’s done I’m  free to remove the logic board from the main body. Removing the logic board also  gives me access to the fan,   a component that can benefit from a regular clean,   and can fail over time. Having it buried  under the logic board is a poor design choice. That goes doubly so for the ports. I love that  each port is modular and individually replaceable,   but it’s just not fun having to go through  so much hardware just to get each one. We’ve   seen plenty of hardware on the market  that prioritises port replacements,   there’s really no reason  why Apple can’t do the same. Next I’ll need to remove the screen starting with  the screws securing the hinge brackets which are   both stuck behind the antenna bracket, so that  component also needs to come out. The antenna   bracket removal is a tedious affair, especially  where the multiple tiny P2 screws are concerned. With the hinge brackets out, I now have  access to the hinge screws which release   the screen. Afterall, no battery replacement  is complete without removing the single most   delicate and expensive component on a  device, 2nd only to the logic board. Thankfully it’s not a terribly complicated  process. And with the screen away,   I can now turn my attention  to the Touch ID hardware. The Touch ID sensor is held in by a few screws  and slides out from underneath the case. The last component to come out in our teardown is  the trackpad which is a straightforward process,   the only tricky part is remembering the  spacers that sit around each screw pillar. Now, finally, after taking apart every  single thing inside the MacBook Pro,   I’m at the point where the Apple repair manual   considers my disassembly process  for a battery replacement complete. This is why selling a battery preinstalled  in a chassis is a bad idea. Because guess   what happens now? That’s right, I have to  reassemble everything I just took apart,   but in the new chassis. Sheesh! No thank you. Repairability Assessment and Score The MacBook Pro M5s aren’t groundbreaking  new devices. It’s an iterative upgrade,   and very marginally so, and that goes  for the repairability of the device too. It’s really nice to see a very simple thing  like bringing all the battery pull tabs in   reach without having to remove the trackpad,  so if Apple ever decides to release the   battery as a standalone part that repair would be  simplified. title: Gaming on a $4000 Macbook Pro - What No One Tells You
transcript: So, this is Apple's most powerful laptop, the M4 Max MacBook Pro. This thing costs $4,000 on Apple's website. And to be honest, for working is actually worth every single penny. But I always wondered, can this $4,000 laptop actually game? Well, I tested it and the results are not what I was expecting at all. Now, in terms of specs, this laptop has the M4 Max chip, the most powerful Apple currently offers. It's got a 16 core CPU, 12 performance cores, and four efficiency cores, and a 40 core GPU, and 48 GB of LPDDR5X unified memory. Now, we also need a baseline if we're going to test this laptop just to see where it sits at. And for that, we're going to use my main gaming rig. It's got an Intel i900 K, an RTX 480 Super, and 64 GB of DDR4 RAMs. It's basically a powerhouse, though, if I'm being honest, it's a little dusty. I haven't used it much because I'm in the middle of building a new rig. But if you calculate the retail price of these components when they were brand new and the inflated price I paid for the GPU, it comes out between $3,000 and $3,500. As for the monitor for the PC, I'm using this ultra budget 32 in 1440p panel from LG Ultra Gear. It's got 180 hertz and one millisecond response time. Not the best, but it will do the job for now. Now, before we jump into any game, I wanted to do some benchmarks. And for this, I used 3D Mark, a very popular benchmarking tool out there. The first test was the Steel Nomad, which is a non rate tracing benchmark for high-end gaming PCs. The MacBook scored above 3700, averaging 37 FPS in 4K. This test was done when the laptop was disconnected from the power source. Then I plugged it in. This time the test came out slightly worse, just shy of 3700, averaging again 37 FPS in 4K. This was shocking. I mean, a normal gaming laptop could never reach the same performance unplugged. Like, that's insane. So, from here on out, all the MacBook tests were done plugged in just to keep everything consistent. The gaming PC, however, scored above 6,400 with an average of 64 FPS in 4K, almost 3,000 more than the MacBook. I mean, I was expecting it to be more powerful, but not with this much. We are talking about almost the same $4,000 here. Then I did a custom Steel Nomad test at 1440p resolution as well and MacBook scored on average 68 FPS whereas the PC an average 122 FPS. Next up was the rate tracing test. I ran a quick benchmark on the MacBook only with the lightweight solar bay test which is made to test lightweight rateracing performance. And the reason why I did this only on the MacBook is because I was honestly expecting it to struggle. But to my surprise, it pulled a staggering 119 average FPS in 4K and 239 FPS in 1440p. Wow. Okay, now it's getting serious. Now, because this test was such a success for the MacBook, I then moved on to the extreme solar bay test, which is a heavy rateracing test for high-end gaming PCs. And the MacBook scored 40 average FPS in 4K, and the gaming PC scored 81 average FPS. So, here the gaming PC has double the performance of the MacBook. It's not looking good for the MacBook, I'm going to be honest. Now, benchmarks are one thing, but how does the MacBook actually handle games? And quick note here, none of the games which I'll show you here has frame gen or upscaling enabled since we are measuring raw hardware power. This is really important. So no DLSS, no FSR, no nothing. The problem is there are not that many games for MacBooks. Roughly a third of my Steam library wasn't compatible on the Mac. So I chose five games that do work. And I know there are other ways to make Windows games compatible with Mac and it has different workarounds, but I wanted to see how the base experience is without any fiddling around. So, Bolatro, a small lightweight game, few megabytes in fact, runs on basically anything, but somehow the Mac struggled. Mac has this weird resolution, so I can't set it to exactly 4K or 1440p. So, at near 4K resolution, it only pulled 40 to 50 FPS. VSYNC disabled. Of course, my PC in 1440p, since I only have a 1440p monitor, that's the max I can set it was above 700 FPS. The difference here is crazy. I was expecting the MacBook getting boltro above three-digit numbers, but I guess I was wrong. Next, Dave the Diver. Also a lightweight game to run. Turns out this game is probably locked at 60 fps because the MacBook in almost 1440p was getting around 53 FPS and my gaming PC in 1440p around 5560FPS. However, the MacBook was noticeably stuttering. The only way I can describe this in every 5 seconds or so there was a micro stutter in the game. Like it wasn't a smooth experience at all. It is what it is. Onto the next one. Outlast. Now, this was the most interesting and disappointing at the same time. This game came out in 2013. You'd expect this game to fly on the MacBook. Well, not really. In almost 1440p, it only pulled 20 to 22 FPS, sometimes dipping as low as 9 FPS. And no matter what graphics settings I used, it didn't have any effect on the performance. Mind you, I played Outlast when I was a kid on literal toaster laptops, so the MacBook has no excuse here. This was so disappointing that I didn't even wanted to compare this to my PC. So, yeah, Outlast 22 FPS. Then, Resident Evil 4 remake. Even though the Mac supports this game, my Steam version just wasn't available for some reason. So, I went on the App Store and downloaded the demo. All said and done, one problem, though. This was the error message I received when I booted into the game. iCloud is not currently available. So you tell me for one, my Steam version is not available on Mac cuz why not? And two, a single player game needs iCloud to run. Personally, I'm not a fan of iCloud. I have it disabled. But then I guess I won't play Resident Evil 4 remake. Well, whatever. Maybe Cyberpunk will be better. Well, Cyberpunk kept crashing when I wanted to load into my saves. According to somebody on the internet, I have to clear the preferences folder on the Mac, and this actually did the trick. I was able to finally load into the game. We are using the high preset graphics settings with the only exception being the field of view is turned up to 100 and motion blur disabled. At near 1440p resolution, the MacBook was getting 59 average FPS with ray tracing enabled, 17 average FPS, and with path tracing enabled, 9.8 average FPS. The gaming PC, however, was pulling with the same high settings, 118 average FPS, with rate tracing, 63 average FPS, and with path tracing, 35 average FPS. Cyberpunk without any rate tracing on the Mac actually feels playable. The FPS isn't as stable, but at least it's playable. However, introducing rate tracing or even path tracing will impact input lag. With patch tracing enabled, it had a visually noticeable input lag. Like, it's almost a full second of delay from when you press a button and when it shows up on the screen. And obviously with 10 FPS and path tracing isn't really playable. But on a positive note, the MacBook's fans have handled all these games and tests like a champ. The FPS might not be as good, but the fan noise actually was incredibly low compared to other devices. For example, the fans on the MacBook Pro rarely kick in when I'm editing. And when I tried Cyberpunk with path tracing, it was still at a manageable level. So, for reference, my ROG Alli X gets way louder when I'm playing games. However, for $4,000, I could build at least two or three PCs that would perform better in any of these games we tested today and even outperform the M4 Max MacBook Pro in gaming, of course. I think the tests today just show how different each device can be from one another. This wasn't a MacBook versus gaming PC video because I was expecting roughly these results. And although the MacBook might not be the best choice for gaming, because let's be honest, who buys a MacBook for just gaming? But for productivity and working, it's hands down the best machine I've had out of all PCs that I own so far. It's easy to bring it with you while traveling and incredibly powerful in creative apps. Without a question, for working, I would always choose a Mac.

The A18 Pro chip is about 40% slower than Apple’s M4 chip, but its multi-core CPU performance is nearly identical to the M1 chip in the 2020 MacBook Air. The current 13-inch MacBook Air starts at $999 in the US, but Apple is still selling the 2020 MacBook Air with the M1 chip for $649 through Walmart. It's also unclear how much RAM the rumored MacBook will pack, as the A18 Pro chip only has 8GB of RAM. In addition to the internal specs, Co said the device is expected to feature an ultra-thin and light design and could come in silver, blue, pink, and yellow . The analyst said the new MacBook will enter mass production towards the end of Q4 2025 or early Q1 2026. The timelines suggest the device could still be announced before the end of the year. However, it's unclear whether Apple has any other announcements planned for 2025, although it's not uncommon for the company to announce new products in late October and even November.

title: M5 MacBook Pro: Pushing the limits at 99°C, but a cooler performer than the M4
transcript: M5 MacBook Pro single fan cooler unable to prevent thermal throttling. Chipset can reach 99° C when under load, but runs cooler than M4. Apple's shift towards its in-house chipsets means that machines like the M5 MacBook Pro will not have to deal with Intel processors overheating the moment you reach the home screen. But that does not mean the company does not continue to cut corners on cooling solutions. The latest example comes from benchmarking the M5, and when the new SoC is pushed a little hard, it can reach 99° C. Thankfully, it runs significantly cooler than the M4, concluding Apple has made those minor thermal adjustments while maintaining the same chassis. M4 MacBook Pro effortlessly crosses the 100° C threshold, while the M5 MacBook Pro keeps it under that thermal ceiling despite both machines sporting the same cooler design. Both the 14-in M5 MacBook Pro and 14in M4 MacBook Pro were pitted against one another in an in-depth video posted by YouTube channel Max Tech. While the M5 Pro and M5 Max models, which are reportedly arriving early next year, will likely be equipped with a dual fan cooling solution. Apple continues to incorporate its base versions with just a single fan and single heat pipe that helps to transfer heat. But looking at the results below, this solution is inefficient in keeping those temperatures down. When running Cinebench 2024, Max Tech found that this cooling solution could not keep up with the M5. But it should also be noted that this test pretty much pushes chipsets to their limit, meaning that if there is not a robust heat sink screwed to the laptop, it will most likely thermal throttle. Fortunately, the positive outcome from this test is that the M5 runs much cooler than the M4 while drawing slightly more power despite both machines having the exact same cooling solution. Looking at this behavior, we can come up with two possibilities. Either Apple has tweaked the fan to ramp up more aggressively in response to higher temperatures, or it has resorted to employing a superior thermal paste like the PTM 7950, which in several cases outperforms liquid metal. You can take a look at the image below along with the comparison details. M4 temperatures when running Cinebench 2024 multi-core. Core average 100.9° C. Core max 114° C. Core minimum 94° C. Package power 18.4W. M5 temperatures when running Cinebench 2024 multi-core. Core average 98.95° C. Core max 99° C. Core minimum 99° C. Package power 21.81W. Considering that the M5 MacBook Pro obtained a massive frame rate boost when running Cyberpunk 2077 with ray tracing enabled. It is impressive that a demanding game would probably have pushed those temperatures further since the CPU and GPU were working in unison. Still, the Apple silicon kept pushing along. Assuming you already own a 14-in M4 MacBook Pro and do not wish to upgrade to the M5 MacBook Pro, whose base model is currently listed for $1,584 on Amazon for the space black finish, but want lower temperatures, you can invest in some PTM 7950. The large thermal pad can be purchased for $35.99 and can be utilized multiple times for successive applications. But you should know how to disassemble and assemble a MacBook Pro before proceeding.
